Detecting invariant expanding cones for generating word
sets to identify chaos in piecewise-linear maps.
D.J.W. Simpson
School of Fundamental Sciences
Massey University
Palmerston North
New Zealand
October 19, 2020
Abstract
We show how the existence of three objects, вД¶trap, W, and C, for a continuous
piecewise-linear map f on RN, implies that f has a topological attractor with a positive
Lyapunov exponent.
First, вД¶trap вКВRN is trapping region for f.
Second, W is a

Second, W is a
пђБnite set of words that encodes the forward orbits of all points in вД¶trap. Finally, C вКВ
TRN is an invariant expanding cone for derivatives of compositions of f formed by the
words in W. We develop an algorithm that identiпђБes these objects for two-dimensional
homeomorphisms comprised of two aпђГne pieces.
The main eпђАort is in the explicit
construction of вД¶trap and C. Their existence is equated to a set of computable conditions
in a general way.

in a general way.
This results in a computer-assisted proof of chaos throughout a
relatively large regime of parameter space. We also observe how the failure of C to
be expanding can coincide with a bifurcation of f. Lyapunov exponents are evaluated
using one-sided directional derivatives so that forward orbits that intersect a switching
manifold (where f is not diпђАerentiable) can be included in the analysis.

Introduction

1
Introduction
Piecewise-linear maps form canonical representations of nonlinear dynamics and provide eпђАec-
tive models of diverse physical systems [10]. Much work has been done to identify properties
that imply a piecewise-linear map has a chaotic attractor.
These include Markov parti-
tions [15], homoclinic connections [26], and situations where the dynamics is eпђАectively one-
dimensional [21]. In the context of ergodic theory, an attractor with an absolutely continuous

invariant measure exists for piecewise-expanding maps [7, 35] and piecewise-smooth maps
with certain expansion properties [31, 38]. However, when applied to the two-dimensional
border-collision normal form (2d BCNF) such properties have only been veriпђБed over regions
1

of parameter space that are small in comparison to where numerical simulations suggest
chaotic attractors actually exist [12, 13]. To address this issue we use invariant expanding
cones to bound Lyapunov exponents. We show that this appears to be a highly eпђАective
method for identifying chaotic attractors in a formal way.
For a continuous map f on RN, the Lyapunov exponent ќї(x, v) characterises the asymp-
totic rate of separation of the forward orbits of arbitrarily close points x and x + ќіv:

then immediately we have ќї(x, v) > 0 for any v вИИC. This idea is attributed to Alekseev [1]
(see [5, 37]) and is useful for establishing splitting and hyperbolicity in smooth dynamical
systems [8, 27, 34].
Condition (1.3) is rather strong as it requires C to be invariant and expanding for every
matrix in the possibly inпђБnite set M. But if f is piecewise-linear then M contains only as
many matrices as pieces in the map. For this reason invariant expanding cones are perfectly

suited, and perhaps under-utilised, for analysing piecewise-linear maps. Invariant cones were
central to MisiurewiczвАЩs strategy for establishing hyperbolicity and transitivity in the Lozi
map [26].
More recently in [16] an invariant expanding cone was constructed for the 2d
BCNF to пђБnally prove the widely held conjecture that a chaotic attractor exists throughout
a physically-important parameter regime RBYG that was пђБrst highlighted in [4].

In this paper we present a simple but powerful generalisation of the above approach and
use it to show that chaotic attractors persist beyond RBYG, in fact in some places right up
to where there exist stable low-period solutions.
The idea is to let M consist of certain
products of the Ai, rather than of the Ai themselves. Each product M вИИM is characterised
by a word W connecting its constitute matrices to the pieces of f. As long as the set of all

such words W generates the symbolic itinerary of the forward orbit of x, then again (1.3)
implies ќї(x, v) > 0. This approach is quite пђВexible because there is a large amount of freedom
in our choice of the set of words W.
To prove f has a chaotic attractor one needs to identify a trapping region вД¶trap вКВRN
(which ensures there exists a topological attractor), a set of words W that generates the
symbolic itineraries for all x вИИвД¶trap, and an invariant expanding cone C for the matrices

corresponding to the words in W. Below we пђБnd these objects for the 2d BCNF. More
precisely, we propose a way by which вД¶trap and C can be constructed for a particular word set
W and prove that all three objects have the required properties if a certain set of computable
2

conditions are met. While, for a given combination of parameter values, these conditions
could be checked by hand, it is more appropriate to check them numerically.
Below we
formulate this as an algorithm (Algorithm 7.1).
The remainder of this paper is organised as follows. We start in ¬І2 by showing where
Algorithm 7.1 detects a chaotic attractor in a typical two-dimensional slice of the parameter
space of the 2d BCNF. Then in ¬І3 we clarify technical features mentioned above (cones,

words, trapping regions, etc) and express ќї(x, v) in terms of one-sided directional derivatives
in order to accommodate points whose forward orbits intersect a switching manifold (where
Df is not deпђБned). The result ќї(x, v) > 0 is formalised by Theorem 3.2 for N-dimensional,
continuous, piecewise-linear maps with two pieces. The theorem is framed in terms of W-
recurrent sets (these are sets to which forward orbits return following one or more words in

W). Such sets provide a practical way by which the approach can be applied to concrete
examples, and in ¬І4 we show they imply that W generates symbolic itineraries as needed.
Here we also characterise the matrices Ai in the expression (1.2). This is quite subtle because
if f i(x) lies on a switching manifold then Ai depends on v as well as x. Section 4 concludes
with a proof of Theorem 3.2.
In subsequent sections we work to apply this methodology to the 2d BCNF. In ¬І5 we

consider sets of 2 √Ч 2 matrices and devise a set of conditions for the existence of an invariant
expanding cone. In ¬І6 we connect consecutive points of an orbit to construct a polygon вД¶.
We then identify conditions implying вД¶is forward invariant and can be perturbed into a
trapping region вД¶trap. In ¬І7 we state Algorithm 7.1 and prove its validity. Here we comment
further on the application of the algorithm to the 2d BCNF and discuss instances in which

failure of the algorithm coincides with a bifurcation of f at which the chaotic attractor is
destroyed. Concluding remarks are provided in ¬І8.

8
Discussion
We have presented a general method by which one can prove, possibly with computer assis-
tance, that a piecewise-linear map has a chaotic attractor. We applied the method to the 2d
BCNF and found a chaotic attractor throughout a parameter regime that, unlike the logis-

tic family for example, does not contain periodic windows. Such robust chaos is typical for
piecewise-linear maps and for this reason piecewise-linear maps are desirable in applications
that use chaos such as chaos-based cryptography [20].
In our implementation we considered only one approach for the construction of вД¶trap and
only word sets of the form (2.3). There is considerable room to generalise these, such as by
deпђБning вД¶trap be to the union of a polygon and its images under f [33].

A major next step would be the application of this method to families of higher-dimensional
maps, such the N-dimensional border-collision normal form. Results of this nature have al-
ready been achieved in [11, 14]. To construct a trapping region and a cone it may be helpful
to work with convex polytopes [2]. It would also be useful to obtain a converse to Theorem
3.2: if f has a topological attractor with a positive Lyapunov exponent, must some вД¶trap,
W, and C (satisfying the required properties) exist?
